title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Title: “Investigating The Health Insurance Coverage of the US Population Across Poverty Level” Olivia and Masha
Description: This project will look at the health insurance status of the US population by state. We will explore the realtionships between status (below the poverty line, in the millitary, gender…etc) and heathcare coverage (medicaid, uninsured, insured by employer…etc).
research questions: How strong is the correlation between poverty level and medicare coverage? Are there groups more likely to be uninsured? Does race/ethnicity have an impact on healthcare coverage?
the reasons/inspirations behind choosing the topic: We are both interested in healthcare disparities within the US and especially how people are affected witht the new cuts to social prgrams such as medicare/medicaid.
one potential data set that can be used to answer the research questions: There are multiple at this link: https://www.kff.org/state-category/health-coverage-uninsured/ and also on the US census website
communication plan: we will communicate through email, and the expected response time will be 24 hours. Right now, meeting times will be 10:45 on wednesday, but subject to change.
roles: Masha-point of contact and style lead, Olivia-technical lead
conflict resolution: we will have clear communication, if the deadline is missed, it will be made up, and if the team is split on something, the team lead responsible for the area of conflict will take the lead (ex: on a technical disagreement, Olivia is the lead and we will do her way.)
a rough implementation plan: see github (this section to be edited once we have more specific tasks)

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text: “The Differences in How CNN, MSNBC, and FOX Cover the News” (The Pudding, 2018) Summary This report by The Pudding does a good job at telling a data story; it avoids overwhelming the reader by breaking down the data and visualizations in annotated chunks. It unpacks the data in layers, building upon understanding of the previous visualization to inform the next one. The authors are trying to get the reader to understand how this kind of bias works through data; but to be convincing they have to take it one step at a time. The story starts by establishing the different networks, and how they could have different word usages (ex: stations say the names of their reporters the most often). These structured examples provide intuition on the visualization itself, and help the reader understand what the placement of the actual data points mean. The next step is to provide case studies within the visualization (ex: who talked the most about natural disasters). Once the reader has understood how different networks could provide biases, the story moves one step further; while stations may use the same words, it does not mean that they are telling the same story. The report then introduces contextual framing by analyzing words commonly paired with others (ex: all networks say “gun,” but Fox pairs it with “control,” CNN/MSNBC pair it with “violence”). Each ‘section’ of the report has an open ended interactive section for the reader, to test their understanding, and apply what they have just learned to topics not explicitly mentioned in the report. One possible limitation may be that the report relies heavily on interactive elements, while this makes it engaging, it may be harder to fully understand if the reader does not actively explore the visualizations.
